old_claim.note: Toll Roads & Bridges provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because border and port customs posts are tariff and inspection institutions, while the rescoped toll node is Roman road-maintenance funding.

source_shape.source_locator: Road maintenance section describing curatores viarum, contracts, maintenance work, and budgets from taxes, tolls, and patronage.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports tolls as one funding input for Roman road maintenance under curatores viarum, not as prerequisite infrastructure for customs checkpoints.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old edge conflated two fiscal mechanisms. Customs posts can collect tariffs at borders or ports without depending on the separate Roman road-funding machinery now represented by toll_roads_and_bridges.

ontology_before: Border customs posts depended on a broad toll-road node whose scope overlapped road funding and bridge tolling.
ontology_after: Border customs posts keep currency and public records as administrative enablers; road-maintenance funding remains in the transport-infrastructure lane.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: The graph no longer routes customs administration through Roman road-funding infrastructure.
would_reject_if: The dependent node were narrowed to a customs post located specifically on a Roman toll road.
A source showed the first scoped customs posts emerged from road-toll institutions rather than tariff administration.
A future split introduced a separate road-toll checkpoint node that correctly depends on toll funding.
short_reason: Customs checkpoints are tariff institutions, not downstream products of road-maintenance toll funding.
